narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 2.80, LSAT score of 36, upper-middle family income, and your Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings of 3 and 1, respectively, along with your plan to attend full-time. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on patterns from thousands of past students, estimated a 73% chance that you would fail the bar on your first attempt—high enough to lead to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes candidates likely to pass and succeed in practice. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which placed you in the bottom tenth of your peers that year and strongly signaled lower academic momentum compared to typical students averaging around the middle of their classes. Next in influence was your LSAT score of 36, slightly below the dataset average of 36.7, which pulled somewhat in the positive direction by suggesting decent aptitude but not enough to offset other concerns. Your Year 1 decile of 3, also below the average of about 5.7, added further weight toward the failure prediction by highlighting early struggles relative to most applicants. While your upper-middle income and full-time status align with the majority of students (over 90% go full-time), they didn't sway the outcome much here. In comparison to typical students who pass the bar, your grades in later years stand out as the biggest gap, as stronger performers usually climb into higher deciles. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Hispanic man with clear drive to pursue law—many in similar backgrounds succeed with targeted improvements. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it above average, strengthening your academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong letters highlighting your resilience. Reapplying after addressing these could shift the model's view, and exploring schools with holistic reviews might value your unique story more. You're capable of turning this around—focus on building that upward trajectory.
